mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses aesthetic experience, apprehension, appreciation, and taste -- the core of the section's treatment of experiencing artworks (A).
Covers criteria of evaluation including aesthetic, formal, and social criteria, and the practice of art criticism (B.1, B.2).
Explores aesthetic criteria for evaluating art and the philosophical basis of taste, relevant to appreciation and evaluation (A.3, B.1).
Directly addresses interpretation of meaning in texts and artworks, central to the section's treatment of interpretation and meaning in art (A.2).
Covers critical methods -- analytical, interpretive, and descriptive -- as applied to literary works, and diverse critical approaches (B.2.b, B.2.d).
Addresses scholarship in the arts and its relation to other humanistic disciplines including history and archaeology (C).
Directly relevant to symbol and myth in the arts as aspects of meaning and interpretation (A.2.b).
Covers mythological archetypes and symbols across cultures as they appear in artistic traditions (A.2.b).
Addresses evaluation criteria related to social, moral, and political significance, and the Frankfurt School's critical approach to art (B.1, B.2.d).
Barthes's semiotic and structural analysis of cultural texts is a major critical method for interpreting meaning in art (A.2.a, B.2.b).
Covers deconstructive and poststructuralist critical approaches to interpreting texts and artworks (B.2.d).
Adorno's aesthetic theory addresses art's social functions and the culture industry, relevant to criticism criteria and the interaction of art with social institutions (B.1, D.1).
Addresses the art market, art world institutions (museums, biennials), and the economics of contemporary art (E, G.1).
Directly covers copyright, piracy, and protection of artists' rights -- central to remuneration, rights, and fraudulence in the arts (E.4, E.5).
Directly relevant to social control of art through censorship and regulation (D.2).
Covers journalistic criticism as a critical style and the media's role in public reception of art (B.2.c).
Explores the experience of reading as aesthetic apprehension and the role of libraries in preserving and disseminating literary works (A.1, G.1).
Explores how imagination operates in aesthetic experience and the apprehension and interpretation of artworks (A.1, A.2).
Relevant to the training and work of the artist, professionalism, and the conditions of creative work (F).
Relevant to the section's discussion of arts in education and aesthetic education (D.5).
Addresses the role of electronic media, sound recording, and streaming in disseminating musical works (G.4, D.4).
Covers cinema's role as mechanical/electronic media for disseminating art, including censorship and technology's impact (D.2, D.4, G.4).
Directly relevant to the role of oral tradition and archives in preserving artistic works (G.1, G.5).
Addresses cross-cultural scholarly and critical methods in the arts and the relation of art scholarship to other humanistic disciplines (C).
Covers the role of industry and commerce in producing and disseminating designed objects as art (G.3, D.1).
Explores art's interaction with modern technology, politics, and new media, relevant to the nonaesthetic context of art (D.1, D.4).
